data_in.description: standard_data.csv containing Component.RT, Base.Peak.MZ, Component.Area, Compound.Name, Match.Factor, and File.Name columns

data_out.description: Comparison table (CSV format) with two columns: Match.Factor threshold and count of unique compounds retained

domain_knowledge: Match.Factor is a spectral library matching confidence score in GC/LC-MS data; higher values indicate stronger spectral similarity between observed and reference spectra.

Match.Factor thresholds of 65, 80, and 90 represent progressively stringent quality cutoffs; the text demonstrates that increasing the threshold reduces retained compounds but improves data quality by removing 'junk' compounds.
In automated GC/LC-MS processing, Match.Factor filtering is applied upstream of compound annotation to exclude low-confidence identifications before downstream analysis.

Unique compound enumeration must account for the fact that the same chemical may appear multiple times in raw data (across different retention times or file replicates) but should be counted only once per filter condition.

direct_checks: verify file standard_data.csv exists in github:castratton__uafR repository
verify row_count_equals for compounds with Match.Factor >= 65 against COND-mf65 output
verify row_count_equals for compounds with Match.Factor >= 80 against COND-mf80 output
verify row_count_equals for compounds with Match.Factor >= 90 against COND-mf90 output
verify output table contains exactly three rows (one per threshold: 65, 80, 90)
verify output table contains columns for threshold value and retained compound count
verify retained_count(>=90) <= retained_count(>=80) <= retained_count(>=65) (monotonic decrease with stricter threshold)
script_runs: R script applying Match.Factor filters to standard_data.csv and producing comparison table executes without error
expert_review: assess whether the Match.Factor thresholds (65, 80, 90) are scientifically appropriate and well-justified for mass spectrometry compound identification quality control
evaluate whether the comparison table format and labeling are clear and suitable for presenting filtering results

cmd: data <- read.csv('{inputs.raw_compound_data}'); thresholds <- c(65, 80, 90); results <- data.frame(Match.Factor.Threshold = paste0('>= ', thresholds), Unique.Compounds.Count = sapply(thresholds, function(t) length(unique(data$Compound.Name[data$Match.Factor >= t])))); write.csv(results, 'match_factor_comparison.csv', row.names = FALSE)

task_description: Filter compound data from standard_data.csv by three Match.Factor thresholds (≥65, ≥80, ≥90) and produce a comparison table showing the number of query chemicals retained under each condition.

task_inputs.label: Standard data CSV with compound names and Match.Factor scores

task_objective: Filter standard_data.csv by Match.Factor thresholds (≥65, ≥80, ≥90) and generate a comparison table showing retained compound counts for each condition.

task_outputs.label: Match.Factor threshold vs. retained compound count comparison table

title: Analyze the effect of Match.Factor threshold on query chemical set size using standard_data

Synthesis grounding: the following tools/outputs were NOT found in the source paper and are inferred — verify before use: Comparison table (CSV format) with two columns: Match.Factor threshold and count of unique compounds retained.
workflow_description: 1. Load standard_data.csv into R and extract the Compound.Name and Match.Factor columns. 2. Apply three independent filters: Match.Factor >= 65, Match.Factor >= 80, and Match.Factor >= 90, retaining unique compound names for each filter. 3. Count the number of unique compounds retained under each threshold condition (COND-mf65, COND-mf80, COND-mf90). 4. Construct a summary table with three rows (one per threshold) and two columns (threshold value and compound count). 5. Save the comparison table as a CSV file.
